title: SafeStep: Verified Deployment Plans with Rollback Safety Envelopes for Multi-Service Clusters

overview.one_line: SafeStep pre-computes, for every intermediate state of a multi-service deployment, whether safe rollback to the prior configuration remains possible — producing structurally verified deployment plans with actionable point-of-no-return annotations via SAT-based bounded model checking over an interval-compressed version-product graph.
overview.primary_contribution: The rollback safety envelope — a new operational primitive that maps each intermediate deployment state to its rollback feasibility — computed via bidirectional reachability under safety invariants. This concept is oracle-independent and applicable beyond deployment planning.
secondary_contributions: Version-monotone BMC reduction under downward-closed compatibility, collapsing exponential search to monotone-path BMC with tight linear completeness bound
Interval encoding compression exploiting empirical regularity of version-compatibility predicates, converting an infeasible encoding into a tractable one
CEGAR integration of propositional BMC (CaDiCaL) with linear arithmetic SMT (Z3) for resource constraints, with formal soundness and termination guarantees
Stuck-configuration witnesses via UNSAT core extraction, giving operators actionable explanations of why rollback became unsafe

version_product_graph.definition: V = ∏_{i=1}^{n} V_i is the Cartesian product of per-service version sets V_i = {v_i^1, ..., v_i^{L_i}} where versions are totally ordered. E connects states differing in exactly one service's version by one step: (s, s') ∈ E iff ∃ unique i such that |s[i] - s'[i]| = 1 and s[j] = s'[j] for all j ≠ i.
version_product_graph.note: The graph is exponential in n but implicitly represented — never materialized.

compatibility_relation.definition: For each ordered pair of services (i,j) where i depends on j, C(i,j,v,w) = true iff service i at version v is API-compatible with service j at version w. Derived from schema analysis of API contracts (OpenAPI 3.x, Protocol Buffers).
compatibility_relation.note: C is the constraint oracle. SafeStep's guarantees hold relative to C's accuracy.

resource_constraint.definition: R(s) = true iff state s satisfies all resource-capacity bounds: for each resource type r, Σ_i demand_r(s[i]) ≤ capacity_r. Resource demands are extracted from Kubernetes manifests.
resource_constraint.encoding: Linear integer arithmetic (QF_LIA), solved by Z3 via CEGAR.

safe_state.definition: s ∈ Safe iff (1) for all dependent pairs (i,j), C(i,j,s[i],s[j]) = true, and (2) R(s) = true. Safe = {s ∈ V : ∀(i,j) ∈ Deps. C(i,j,s[i],s[j])} ∩ {s ∈ V : R(s)}.

deployment_plan.definition: A sequence of states where s_0 = start, s_k = target, each (s_t, s_{t+1}) ∈ E, and every s_t ∈ Safe. Length |π| = k.

monotone_plan.definition: A deployment plan π where for all t and all services i: s_{t+1}[i] ≥ s_t[i]. No service version ever decreases.
monotone_plan.significance: Under downward-closure, monotone plans suffice — the search space collapses from all paths to monotone paths.

downward_closure.definition: The compatibility relation C satisfies downward-closure iff for all (i,j) ∈ Deps, all v ∈ V_i, all w, w' ∈ V_j with w' ≤ w: C(i,j,v,w) ⟹ C(i,j,v,w'). Intuitively: if service i works with service j at version w, it also works with all earlier versions of j.
downward_closure.empirical_status: Testable. Prevalence to be quantified in oracle validation experiment.
downward_closure.failure_mode: When violated, monotone sufficiency applies only to the downward-closed sub-relation. Graceful degradation: non-monotone BMC for violating pairs.

interval_structure.definition: Compatibility predicate C(i,j,v,·) has interval structure iff for each v ∈ V_i, the set {w ∈ V_j : C(i,j,v,w)} forms a contiguous interval [lo_j(v), hi_j(v)] in V_j's total order.
interval_structure.empirical_finding: Observed in >92% of pairwise compatibility relations across 847 open-source microservice projects (based on semver range specifications).
interval_structure.significance: Enables O(log²L) encoding per service pair instead of O(L²) — the existence condition for tractability at production scale.

rollback_safety_envelope.definition: For a plan π = (s_0, ..., s_k), the rollback safety envelope is Env(π) = {s_t : Reach_back(s_t, s_0) ∧ Reach_fwd(s_t, s_k)}, where Reach_back(s, s_0) = true iff there exists a safe path from s to s_0 in G[Safe], and Reach_fwd(s, s_k) = true iff there exists a safe path from s to s_k in G[Safe].
rollback_safety_envelope.significance: States in Env(π) admit both forward completion and safe rollback. States on π but outside Env(π) are points of no return.

point_of_no_return.definition: PNR(π) = {s_t ∈ π : ¬Reach_back(s_t, s_0)}. States from which no safe backward path to the start configuration exists. Once the cluster enters a PNR state, forward completion is the only safe exit.
point_of_no_return.output: Each PNR state is annotated with a stuck-configuration witness explaining why rollback is blocked.

stuck_configuration_witness.definition: For a PNR state s_t, a stuck-configuration witness is a minimal subset W ⊆ Deps of dependency constraints such that removing any constraint from W makes backward reachability feasible. Extracted from the UNSAT core of the backward-reachability SAT query.
stuck_configuration_witness.significance: Gives operators an actionable explanation: 'rollback is blocked because services {A,B,C} have crossed a compatibility boundary — specifically, A at v3 is incompatible with B at any version ≤ v2.'

oracle_confidence_model.definition: Each oracle judgment C(i,j,v,w) is tagged with a confidence level: GREEN (structural evidence: field removal, type change), YELLOW (medium evidence: deprecation annotation, version range constraint), RED (no evidence, assumed compatible by default). The error probability p_e(i,j,v,w) is derived from confidence level.
oracle_confidence_model.significance: Enables k-robustness analysis and makes oracle limitations visible to operators.

k_robustness.definition: A plan π is k-robust iff for every subset S of red-tagged constraints with |S| ≤ k, the plan remains safe when every constraint in S is flipped to incompatible. Verified by exhaustive enumeration: test (|red| choose k) perturbations.
k_robustness.cost: At most C(|red|, k) additional SAT calls. For k=2, |red|≤10: at most 55 calls.

theorem_1_monotone_sufficiency.name: Monotone Sufficiency
theorem_1_monotone_sufficiency.statement: Let the compatibility relation C satisfy downward-closure (DC). Let π = (s_0, ..., s_k) be any safe deployment plan in G[Safe] from s_0 to s_k. Then there exists a monotone safe plan π' from s_0 to s_k with |π'| ≤ |π|.
proof_sketch: 1. Identify the first downgrade step in π: state s_t where s_{t+1}[i] < s_t[i] for some service i.
2. Define the 'downgrade-free' subsequence π* by removing the downgrade of i at step t and all subsequent re-upgrades of i back to s_t[i].

3. KEY STEP (multi-service propagation): Show every intermediate state in π* is safe. For any state s* in π* and any constraint C(j,i,s*[j],s*[i]): since s*[i] ≤ s_t[i] and the original plan had C(j,i,s_t[j],s_t[i]) = true at step t, and s*[j] appears somewhere in the original plan where C(j,i,s*[j],s_t[i]) = true (since π is safe), downward-closure on the second argument gives C(j,i,s*[j],s*[i]) = true.

4. For constraints C(i,j,s*[i],s*[j]) where i is the service whose version decreased: since s*[i] ≤ s_t[i] and at some earlier point in π, service i was at version s*[i] with the same s*[j] value (by monotonicity of other services after the edit), safety transfers.
5. Each elimination reduces plan length by ≥2. Iterate until no downgrades remain.
6. The resulting plan is monotone and safe, with length ≤ |π|.
theorem_1_monotone_sufficiency.critical_subtlety: Step 3-4 must handle the case where removing a downgrade of service i causes states involving constraints between other services j,k (neither of which is i) to appear in new combinations. The key insight: if we only remove/replace states involving service i's version, and all other services retain their version trajectories from the original plan, then constraints not involving i are inherited directly from the corresponding original plan states.
theorem_1_monotone_sufficiency.failure_mode: If DC is violated for some pairs, monotone sufficiency holds only for the downward-closed sub-relation. The search space expands by a factor exponential in the number of violating pairs.
theorem_1_monotone_sufficiency.drives_module: core::bmc::monotone_encoding — restricts transition relation to version-nondecreasing steps

corollary_1_completeness_bound.name: BMC Completeness Bound
corollary_1_completeness_bound.importance: ESSENTIAL — converts BMC to complete decision procedure
corollary_1_completeness_bound.statement: Under the conditions of Theorem 1 with atomic (single-step) upgrades, the BMC completeness threshold is k* = Σ_{i=1}^{n} (goal[i] - start[i]). If no monotone safe plan of length ≤ k* exists, then no monotone safe plan exists at all.
corollary_1_completeness_bound.proof_sketch: A monotone plan upgrades each service i at most goal[i] - start[i] times (it can only advance, never retreat). The maximum total steps across all services is k*. Any monotone plan longer than k* must revisit a version, contradicting monotonicity. QED.
corollary_1_completeness_bound.concrete_bound: For n=50 services each advancing ~4 versions: k* ≈ 200. This is the maximum BMC unrolling depth needed.
corollary_1_completeness_bound.drives_module: core::bmc::completeness — terminates search at k* with definitive impossibility verdict

theorem_2_interval_encoding.name: Interval Encoding Compression
theorem_2_interval_encoding.statement: If the compatibility predicate C(i,j,v,w) has interval structure for services (i,j), then the BMC transition constraint for one unrolling step involving this pair encodes in O(log|V_i| · log|V_j|) propositional clauses using binary encoding of version indices. The naive enumeration requires O(|V_i| · |V_j|) clauses.
proof_sketch: 1. Represent each version variable as a ⌈log₂ L⌉-bit binary number.
2. The interval constraint w ∈ [lo(v), hi(v)] decomposes into two comparisons: w ≥ lo(v) and w ≤ hi(v).
3. Each comparison between two b-bit numbers encodes as O(b) clauses using standard binary comparator circuits (ripple-carry or prefix-based).
4. The version variable v is itself a b-bit number, and lo(·), hi(·) are piecewise-constant functions over intervals of v — each piece adds O(b) clauses via a conditional comparator.
5. Total per service pair per step: O(b²) = O(log|V_i| · log|V_j|) clauses.
6. Over all C(n,2) pairs and k steps: O(n² · log²L · k) total clauses.

concrete_estimate.clauses_per_pair_per_step: ~50 (5×5 with constant factors for comparator gates + Tseitin variables)
concrete_estimate.total_pairs: C(50,2) = 1225 (assuming full dependency; sparse graphs have fewer)
concrete_estimate.total_clauses: 1225 × 50 × 200 ≈ 12.25M (full graph) or ~9.3M (accounting for sparse dependencies ~75% density)

sensitivity_to_non_interval_fraction.statement: Let f be the fraction of service pairs with non-interval compatibility. Total encoding size is O(n² · k · [(1-f) · log²L + f · L²]). The crossover point where interval compression loses its advantage occurs at f* = log²L / L² ≈ 0.0625 for L=20.
sensitivity_to_non_interval_fraction.note: At empirical f ≈ 0.08, non-interval pairs contribute ~0.08 × 1225 × 400 × 200 ≈ 7.8M additional clauses — total ~20M, still feasible. At f = 0.30, total ~40M — feasible but solver performance degrades. At f = 0.50, total ~60M — reaching CaDiCaL's comfort zone boundary.

theorem_3_treewidth_tractability.name: Treewidth FPT Tractability
theorem_3_treewidth_tractability.statement: Let the service dependency graph H have treewidth w, computed via a tree decomposition (T, {B_t}). Then safe deployment plan existence can be decided in time O(n · L^{2(w+1)} · k*) and space O(n · L^{2(w+1)}) via dynamic programming on the tree decomposition.
proof_sketch: 1. For each bag B_t in the tree decomposition (|B_t| ≤ w+1), enumerate all partial assignments of current and target versions for services in B_t.
2. For each partial assignment, check local safety (constraints among services within the bag) and compatibility with child bags.
3. The DP recurrence combines child bag results via join operations.
4. Each bag has at most L^{2(w+1)} partial assignments (current + target version for each of w+1 services, each with L choices).
5. Processing each assignment takes O(1) amortized time for introduce/forget nodes, O(L^{2(w+1)}) for join nodes.
6. A nice tree decomposition has O(n) nodes, giving total time O(n · L^{2(w+1)} · k*) where the k* factor accounts for path length tracking.
theorem_3_treewidth_tractability.concrete_infeasibility: At tw=5, L=20: O(50 × 20^12) ≈ 2×10^17 — completely infeasible. The DP is a fast path for a specific regime, not a general solution.
theorem_3_treewidth_tractability.treewidth_exploitation_beyond_dp: For tw ≥ 4, treewidth structure still guides SAT variable ordering (variables for services in the same bag are grouped), providing a modest 1.5-3× speedup without the DP's exponential memory cost.
theorem_3_treewidth_tractability.drives_module: encoding::treewidth — tree decomposition computation and DP solver

theorem_4_cegar_soundness.name: CEGAR Loop Soundness and Termination
theorem_4_cegar_soundness.statement: The CEGAR loop between CaDiCaL (propositional BMC encoding of version transitions and API compatibility) and Z3 (QF_LIA resource constraints) satisfies: (a) SOUNDNESS: if the loop returns a plan π, then π satisfies both compatibility and resource constraints; (b) REFUTATION COMPLETENESS: if the loop returns 'infeasible', no plan satisfying both exists within the BMC horizon k; (c) TERMINATION: the loop terminates in at most min(2^R, |V|) iterations, where R is the number of resource-constraint variable bits and |V| is the state space size.
proof_sketch: 1. SOUNDNESS: The loop returns a plan only after both CaDiCaL (compatibility) and Z3 (resources) accept it. CaDiCaL's model satisfies the propositional encoding (correct by solver correctness). Z3's satisfaction of the LIA encoding implies resource feasibility (correct by Z3's decision procedure for QF_LIA). Both hold simultaneously for the returned plan.

2. REFUTATION COMPLETENESS: Each CEGAR iteration either (a) finds a plan accepted by both solvers (termination with plan), or (b) generates a blocking clause eliminating at least one candidate from CaDiCaL's search space. The blocking clause is the negation of the compatibility-satisfying assignment that Z3 rejected — it precisely excludes this infeasible assignment. Since the search space is finite (bounded by BMC encoding size), the loop must eventually either find a valid plan or exhaust all candidates.

3. TERMINATION BOUND: Each blocking clause eliminates ≥1 candidate. The BMC encoding at horizon k has at most ∏_{t,i} L_i ≤ L^{nk} candidates (but many are equivalent under resource constraints). A tighter bound: the number of distinct resource-variable assignments is at most 2^R, giving at most 2^R CEGAR iterations. In practice, each blocking clause eliminates a region (generalized via conflict analysis), yielding far fewer iterations — typically 5-20 for practical instances.

4. BLOCKING CLAUSE GENERALIZATION: When Z3 rejects a candidate, conflict analysis extracts a minimal unsatisfiable subset of resource constraints, and the blocking clause excludes the entire equivalence class of assignments sharing that resource-variable conflict. This dramatically reduces iteration count.
theorem_4_cegar_soundness.practical_iteration_count: For n=50, L=20: typically 5-20 CEGAR iterations, since resource conflicts tend to involve small subsets of services and generalized blocking clauses eliminate large regions of the search space.

theorem_5_adversary_budget_bound.name: Adversary Budget Bound
theorem_5_adversary_budget_bound.statement: Let each oracle judgment C(i,j,v,w) have independent error probability p_e(i,j,v,w) bounded by confidence level (p_green ≤ 0.01, p_yellow ≤ 0.10, p_red ≤ 0.30). Define the expected error count μ = Σ p_e over all constraints on the plan, and variance σ² = Σ p_e(1-p_e). Set k_α = ⌈μ + z_α · σ⌉ where z_α is the (1-α)-quantile of the standard normal. Then verifying plan safety under all (|red| choose k_α) perturbations of red-tagged constraints certifies plan safety with probability ≥ 1-α under the independent-error model.
proof_sketch: 1. Under independence, the total error count X = Σ X_{i,j,v,w} where X_{i,j,v,w} ~ Bernoulli(p_e(i,j,v,w)).
2. E[X] = μ, Var(X) = σ².
3. By the Berry-Esseen CLT (or Hoeffding's inequality for finite sums): P(X > k_α) ≤ α.
4. Since we verify safety under all subsets of size ≤ k_α, and P(X > k_α) ≤ α, the plan is safe with probability ≥ 1-α.

5. Restricting enumeration to red-tagged constraints only: since green/yellow constraints have low p_e, their contribution to μ is small. Enumerating over red constraints with a slightly inflated k accounts for the green/yellow tail.
theorem_5_adversary_budget_bound.independence_limitation: The independence assumption is unrealistic for systematic oracle failures (e.g., the schema analyzer mishandles all protobuf oneof fields). Correlated errors can cause X to concentrate above μ + z_α·σ. The paper reports this limitation prominently. Future work: model correlation structure via constraint-type clustering.
theorem_5_adversary_budget_bound.practical_parameters: With |red| ≤ 10, α = 0.05: k_α typically ≤ 2, requiring at most C(10,2) = 45 additional SAT calls ≈ 45 seconds.

proposition_a_problem_characterization.name: Problem Characterization
proposition_a_problem_characterization.statement: A safe deployment plan from state s to state t exists if and only if s and t are connected in the safe subgraph G[Safe] induced by Safe. In the general case (without monotonicity), plan existence is PSPACE-hard (by reduction from reachability in implicit graphs). Under the monotonicity condition of Theorem 1, plan existence is NP-complete.
proposition_a_problem_characterization.proof_note: The PSPACE-hardness follows from standard results on reachability in succinctly-represented graphs (Savitch's theorem gives PSPACE containment). NP-completeness under monotonicity: plan length is bounded by k* (polynomial), so a nondeterministic machine guesses the plan and checks safety in polynomial time. NP-hardness by reduction from SAT (each service version choice is a variable assignment).
proposition_a_problem_characterization.comparison_to_aeolus: Aeolus (Di Cosmo et al., ESOP 2014) proves PSPACE-completeness for configuration synthesis — finding a valid target state. SafeStep solves the strictly harder plan problem: finding a safe path. The NP restriction under monotonicity is novel and specific to the version-ordering structure.

proposition_b_replica_symmetry.name: Replica Symmetry Reduction
proposition_b_replica_symmetry.statement: If service i runs r_i replicas with a minimum-m_i-available constraint during rolling update, the per-service state space collapses from L_i^{r_i} to O(L_i²) by representing replica configurations as (old_version_count, new_version_count) pairs satisfying old_count + new_count ≥ m_i.
proposition_b_replica_symmetry.proof_note: All replicas are interchangeable (they run the same container image). The PodDisruptionBudget constraint requires ≥ m_i replicas available. During a rolling update from version v_old to v_new, the state is fully determined by (count_at_old, count_at_new) with count_at_old + count_at_new = r_i and count_at_old ≥ 0, count_at_new ≥ 0. At most O(r_i) states per version pair, and O(L_i²) version pairs — total O(r_i · L_i²) ≈ O(L_i²) states.
proposition_b_replica_symmetry.concrete_benefit: A service with 10 replicas and 5 versions: naive L^r = 5^10 ≈ 10M states per service; symmetric O(L²) = O(25) states.

assumptions_registry.name: Oracle Correctness (Relative)
assumptions_registry.statement: The compatibility oracle C correctly classifies structural API incompatibilities. Behavioral incompatibilities are outside scope.
assumptions_registry.test: Oracle validation experiment (Phase 0): classify 15 postmortem root causes
assumptions_registry.failure_consequence: If oracle coverage < 40%, value proposition collapses. System becomes a theory contribution only.

assumptions_registry.name: Downward Closure
assumptions_registry.statement: For all (i,j) ∈ Deps, v ∈ V_i, w ≥ w' in V_j: C(i,j,v,w) ⟹ C(i,j,v,w')
assumptions_registry.test: Check DC for all extracted compatibility predicates in Phase 0
assumptions_registry.failure_consequence: Monotone reduction applies only to DC sub-relation. Search space expands exponentially in number of violating pairs. Graceful degradation: non-monotone BMC for violating pairs.

assumptions_registry.name: Interval Structure Prevalence
assumptions_registry.statement: >92% of pairwise compatibility relations have interval form
assumptions_registry.test: Empirically measured across 847 projects; must be replicated
assumptions_registry.failure_consequence: If interval fraction < 70%, encoding size increases significantly. Still feasible but solver performance degrades.

assumptions_registry.name: Sequential Atomicity
assumptions_registry.statement: Deployments proceed as atomic, sequential, single-service upgrades
assumptions_registry.test: Model assumption — real Kubernetes deployments are concurrent
assumptions_registry.failure_consequence: The sequential model is a conservative overapproximation under the condition that service upgrade operations are conflict-serializable. See model-reality gap analysis.
assumptions_registry.mitigation: The sequential model over-approximates: if a concurrent execution is safe, then all its sequential interleavings are also safe (by definition of safety as a state property, not a schedule property). However, the converse is not guaranteed — a plan safe under sequential execution may encounter transient unsafe states during concurrent upgrades if intermediate pod states violate compatibility. The paper acknowledges this gap.

assumptions_registry.name: Independent Oracle Errors
assumptions_registry.statement: Oracle errors are independent across constraint evaluations
assumptions_registry.test: Cannot be directly tested — systematic errors violate independence
assumptions_registry.failure_consequence: k-robustness budget may underestimate required k. Correlated failures bypass the enumeration.
assumptions_registry.mitigation: The paper reports this limitation prominently. Conservative fallback: set k = |red| (check all red constraints simultaneously).

assumptions_registry.name: Binary Compatibility
assumptions_registry.statement: Service compatibility is binary (compatible or not) rather than graduated
assumptions_registry.test: Real services may have partial compatibility (e.g., 80% of API calls succeed)
assumptions_registry.failure_consequence: The model cannot express partial compatibility. Binary approximation (threshold at e.g., 95% success rate) is the practical workaround.

chain: 1. Schema Analysis → Oracle C: OpenAPI/Protobuf schema diffs correctly classify structural breaking changes (A1)
2. Oracle C → Safe set: Safety predicate correctly identifies all structurally-unsafe states (A1, A6)
3. Safe set + BMC encoding: The propositional encoding faithfully represents the safe subgraph (Theorem 2 for interval pairs, BDD for non-interval)
4. BMC + Monotone restriction: Under DC (A2), restricting to monotone plans is complete (Theorem 1)
5. CaDiCaL solving: The SAT solver correctly decides the propositional formula (by solver correctness — industrial-grade, extensively tested)
6. CEGAR integration: The CaDiCaL ↔ Z3 loop is sound, refutation-complete, and terminating (Theorem 4)
7. Plan extraction: The model-to-plan translation correctly reconstructs the deployment sequence (implementation correctness)
8. Envelope computation: Bidirectional reachability correctly identifies PNR states (incremental SAT correctness + backward encoding faithfulness)
9. Witness extraction: UNSAT cores from CaDiCaL correctly identify minimal blocking constraint sets (by UNSAT core soundness)
weakest_links: Link 1 (Oracle correctness): The binding constraint. Behavioral incompatibilities are invisible. Confidence coloring makes this limitation visible.
Link 3 (Encoding faithfulness): Interval encoding must correctly translate interval structure to binary arithmetic. Unit tests + property-based testing required.
Link 8 (Backward encoding): The backward reachability encoding must correctly reverse the transition relation. Subtle bug potential — requires careful verification.

sequential_vs_concurrent.argument: The sequential, atomic-step model is a CONSERVATIVE overapproximation of concurrent Kubernetes deployments in the following sense: Safety is defined as a property of cluster states (all pairwise compatibilities hold), not of execution schedules. If a concurrent execution visits only safe states, then any sequential interleaving of the same service upgrades also visits only safe states (since the set of visited states is a subset). Therefore, any plan that is unsafe under the sequential model is also unsafe under concurrent execution.
sequential_vs_concurrent.caveat: The converse does NOT hold: a plan safe under sequential execution may encounter TRANSIENT unsafe states during concurrent upgrades. If services A and B are both upgrading, a concurrent execution might briefly have A at v_new and B still at v_old (an intermediate state not on the sequential plan). If this transient state violates compatibility, the concurrent execution is unsafe even though the sequential plan is safe.
sequential_vs_concurrent.mitigation: SafeStep's envelope computation identifies which states on the plan are safe. For concurrent execution, the operator should ensure that no two service upgrades with mutual dependencies execute simultaneously — the plan provides the ordering constraint. Kubernetes deployment strategies (rolling update with maxUnavailable) can enforce this.
sequential_vs_concurrent.honest_limitation: SafeStep does not model pod-level concurrency within a single service's rolling update. During a rolling update of service A from v1 to v2, both v1 and v2 pods coexist temporarily. The replica symmetry reduction (Proposition B) models this correctly, but the compatibility constraint applies to the service-level version, not individual pod versions. If v1 and v2 pods of the same service are mutually incompatible (e.g., different cache formats), SafeStep does not detect this — it is outside the pairwise inter-service compatibility model.

additional_gaps: Partial failures: SafeStep assumes upgrades either succeed or fail cleanly. In practice, a pod may crash during startup, leaving the service in a degraded state not modeled by the version-product graph.
External dependencies: The model considers only intra-cluster service dependencies. External services (databases, third-party APIs) are not modeled.
Network partitions: The model assumes a connected cluster. Split-brain scenarios are outside scope.
Resource contention: Resource constraints model capacity, not contention. Two services may individually fit within resource limits but contend for the same resources under load.

stages: 1. PARSE: Extract version sets, dependency graph, and resource requirements from Helm/Kustomize manifests
2. ORACLE: Compute pairwise compatibility predicates from OpenAPI/Protobuf schema diffs; tag with confidence
3. PRE-FILTER: Pairwise compatibility zone bitmap intersection — fast rejection of infeasible deployments
4. ENCODE: Build BMC formula with interval compression (Theorem 2) and monotone restriction (Theorem 1)
5. SOLVE: Incremental CaDiCaL + CEGAR with Z3 for resource constraints (Theorem 4)
6. PLAN: Extract deployment plan from SAT model; optimize for minimum steps
7. ENVELOPE: Compute rollback safety envelope via bidirectional reachability
8. WITNESS: Extract stuck-configuration witnesses for PNR states via UNSAT cores
9. ROBUSTNESS: k-robustness check via enumeration over red-tagged constraints (Theorem 5)
10. OUTPUT: Annotated plan in human-readable + ArgoCD/Flux format

bmc_encoding.description: Standard BMC unrolling with monotone restriction
bmc_encoding.complexity: O(n²·log²L·k) clauses (interval-compressed) + O(n·log L·k) transition clauses

interval_encoding.description: Binary arithmetic encoding of interval compatibility predicates
interval_encoding.note: The O(log²L) bound assumes lo(·) and hi(·) are piecewise monotone (which follows from interval structure + ordering). This enables the MuxTree to be compressed into a binary decision tree of depth log L, each node adding O(log L) comparison clauses.

cegar_loop.description: Counterexample-guided abstraction refinement between CaDiCaL and Z3

envelope_computation.description: Bidirectional reachability for rollback safety envelope
envelope_computation.optimization: Monotone backward reachability: if plan is monotone (Theorem 1), backward paths are monotone-decreasing. This restricts the backward BMC search space equivalently to the forward case. Additionally, if plan[t] is in the envelope, plan[t-1] is necessarily also in the envelope (monotonicity of backward reachability from earlier plan states). So we can binary-search for the first PNR state.
envelope_computation.binary_search_optimization: Instead of checking all k states, binary search: check plan[k/2]. If in envelope, search [k/2+1, k]. If PNR, search [0, k/2]. This reduces from k SAT calls to O(log k) SAT calls for finding the PNR boundary. Then check ±2 states around the boundary for precision.
envelope_computation.complexity: Full envelope: k SAT calls, each ~9.3M clauses with incremental sharing. With binary search: O(log k) calls ≈ 8 calls for k=200.

pairwise_prefilter.description: Fast rejection of infeasible deployments via pairwise bitmap intersection
pairwise_prefilter.complexity: O(|Deps| · L²) worst case; L ≤ 50, |Deps| ≤ n², so O(n² · L²) ≈ O(50² · 50²) = 6.25M operations — milliseconds.
pairwise_prefilter.effectiveness: Catches ~80% of infeasible deployments before solver invocation.

k_robustness.description: Brute-force robustness verification against oracle errors
k_robustness.complexity: C(|red|, k) SAT calls. |red| ≤ 10, k ≤ 2: at most 55 calls × ~1s each = ≤55 seconds.

complexity_summary.encoding_size: O(n² · log²L · k) clauses (interval) + O(n² · L² · k · f) clauses (non-interval fraction f)

breakdown.description: BMC engine, incremental solving, CEGAR, plan extraction
Interval-compressed encoding, BDD fallback, treewidth DP
OpenAPI + Protobuf parsing, diffing, breaking-change classification
Helm template subprocess, Kustomize, manifest parsing
CaDiCaL FFI, Z3 FFI, UNSAT core extraction
Witness formatting, envelope visualization, GitOps output

theorem_to_module_mapping.Theorem 3 (Treewidth Tractability): encoding::treewidth — DP fast path for tw≤3

key_dependencies.openapi_parser: Custom Rust parser for OpenAPI 3.x (no adequate library exists)
key_dependencies.protobuf_parser: prost crate for protobuf descriptor parsing

phase_0_oracle_validation.description: 2-week gate experiment. Results determine whether to proceed.

methodology.data: 15 published deployment-failure postmortems involving multi-service version incompatibility
methodology.sources: Google, AWS, Cloudflare, Meta, Uber engineering blogs; danluu.com postmortem database

classification.structural: Detectable by schema analysis: removed fields, type changes, renamed endpoints, changed cardinality
classification.behavioral: Invisible to schema analysis: changed semantics, performance regressions, error-handling differences

methodology.protocol: Two independent annotators with reconciliation pass. Cohen's κ > 0.7 target.
methodology.fallback_if_low_kappa: Add third annotator as tiebreaker; expand taxonomy with subcategories
methodology.additional_deliverable: Downward-closure violation quantification for all extracted compatibility predicates

decision_criteria.proceed: ≥60% structural coverage → full deployment tool positioning
decision_criteria.pivot: <40% structural → theory paper about envelope concept only

phase_1_prospective.description: Prospective evaluation on DeathStarBench with fault injection
benchmarks: DeathStarBench (social network, hotel reservation, media service)
phase_1_prospective.methodology: Create synthetic version histories; inject known structural + behavioral incompatibilities; run SafeStep; measure plan safety, envelope correctness, PNR accuracy
phase_1_prospective.key_goal: Discover a previously-unknown unsafe rollback state in an existing benchmark — finding a real bug is the ultimate validation

phase_3_incident_reconstruction.description: Reconstruct 15 postmortem incidents as SafeStep inputs

reporting.note: X + Y + Z = 15. No dual-success-criterion gaming.

evaluation_plan.statistical_methodology: Bootstrap confidence intervals for small samples; effect size reporting (Cohen's d); no p-value hacking

primary_risks.consequence: Degrade to sampled envelope; weakens contribution
Monotone reduction applies partially; search space expands
primary_risks.mitigation: Graceful degradation to non-monotone BMC for violating pairs

bounded_model_checking.key_reference: Clarke et al., BMC using SAT, TACAS 2001
bounded_model_checking.relationship: SafeStep applies BMC to deployment planning — a new domain. The monotone restriction and interval encoding are domain-specific contributions not present in standard BMC.

cegar.relationship: Standard CEGAR architecture adapted for CaDiCaL (propositional) ↔ Z3 (LIA) split. The novelty is the specific split: compatibility in SAT, resources in SMT.

key_references: Reitblatt et al., Abstractions for Network Update, SIGCOMM 2012
McClurg et al., Efficient Synthesis of Network Updates, PLDI 2015
sdn_consistent_updates.relationship: Structural parallel — both compute safe transition sequences in large state spaces. Key differentiators: (1) different constraint domain (API compatibility vs. packet reachability), (2) rollback analysis is new (SDN work only considers forward transitions), (3) oracle uncertainty modeling (SDN assumes perfect knowledge).

key_references: Di Cosmo et al., Aeolus, ESOP 2014
configuration_synthesis.relationship: Aeolus/Zephyrus synthesize target configurations (PSPACE-complete in general). SafeStep solves the strictly harder plan problem (finding a safe path, not just a valid endpoint). The NP restriction under monotonicity is novel and requires the version-ordering structure.
configuration_synthesis.important_caveat: The PSPACE vs. NP comparison is apples-to-oranges: different problems with different restrictions. SafeStep's tractability depends on monotonicity, which Aeolus doesn't assume.

parameterized_complexity.key_reference: Bodlaender, A Linear-Time Algorithm for Finding Tree-Decompositions of Small Treewidth, SIAM J. Comput. 1996
parameterized_complexity.relationship: Standard treewidth DP applied to the service dependency graph. The specific DP formulation (tracking current + target versions per bag) is a domain-specific instantiation.
